## Web context (cite URLs when used)
- National Sporting Club - Wikipedia (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Sporting_Club): # National Sporting Club. The **National Sporting Club** (**NSC**) was a private members' sporting club founded in London on 5 March 1891 by John Fleming and Arthur Frederick Bettinson. Operating from premises at 43 King Street, Covent Garden, it became the principal centre for elite professional boxing in Britain during the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries. During the early twentieth century, eligibility for British championship contests was effectively limited to white boxers through administrative practice rather than formal statute, a restriction that remained in place for the

- The National Sporting Club - Books (https://www.amazon.com/National-Sporting-Club-Present-Classic/dp/1334227365): This book chronicles the history of the National Sporting Club in London, from 1664 to 1856, and then from its rebirth in 1891 to 1901.
- National Sporting Club (https://www.londonremembers.com/subjects/national-sporting-club): Founded by John Fleming and AF "Peggy" Bettinson. First president Hugh Cecil Lowther, Fifth Earl of Lonsdale. It became the home of modern glove boxing.

rounds.story:
**A Brief Detour into the Hippie Underground** 🚶

Just 3 meters west from the [📍43 King Street](https://www.google.com/maps/search/43+King+Street/@51.5121,-0.123859,17z/) theatre building, we find ourselves at the doorstep of a far wilder era — **Middle Earth**, the 1960s hippie club that once pulsed with psychedelic energy and countercultural rebellion. Though the club itself doesn’t survive in this exact spot, its very existence in 1968 made it an underground jewel in the heart of style-conscious Covent Garden.

To the modern eye, [📍Middle Earth (club)](https://www.google.com/maps/search/Middle+Earth+%28club%29/@51.5121,-0.1239,17z/) is invisible, but it’s the kind of place that still breathes in the air of King Street. Imagine tripping over the same cobblestones while the Velvet Underground played, and acid-fried muralists turned alley walls into living dreams. It wasn’t just a club — it was a portal to a new world of anti-establishment art, radical politics, and experimental music. The National Sporting Club, [📍10 meters away](https://www.google.com/maps/search/National+Sporting Club/@51.5121,-0.1239,17z/) may have been the boxing world’s nerve center a century earlier, but this was its countercultural heir.

The irony? Just steps from this former hippie haven lies [📍Evans Music-and-Supper Rooms](https://www.google.com/maps/search/Evans+Music-and-Supper+Rooms/@51.51199,-0.12387,17z/) — a 19th-century venue that hosted choral singing and Victorian propriety. The alleyways here once switched between hymn-singing and hallucinogenic revelry like it was a regular encore.

Middle Earth didn’t last long, but it left a legacy. Today, its memories exist mostly in the listings of [📖wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Middle_Earth_%28club%29) and the speculative glances of those who wonder what the area was like before tea-sipping replaced tie-dying. As we move on, take a breath — the past and present are just a few steps apart.

claims.claim: Middle Earth was a 1960s hippie club located at 43 King Street, Covent Garden.
claims.evidence: The evidence shows that Middle Earth (club) is listed as a Wikipedia article, 10m from 43 King Street. However, it does not confirm that Middle Earth was located at 43 King Street. The National Sporting Club is mentioned as being at 43 King Street, not Middle Earth.

claims.claim: Middle Earth was an underground jewel in the heart of style-conscious Covent Garden in 1968.
claims.evidence: The evidence confirms that Middle Earth (club) is listed as a Wikipedia article, 10m from 43 King Street, and the story's context aligns with the 1960s countercultural scene in Covent Garden.

claims.claim: The National Sporting Club is located 10 meters away from Middle Earth.
claims.evidence: The evidence shows that both the National Sporting Club and Middle Earth (club) are listed as 10m from 43 King Street, suggesting they are in close proximity.

claims.claim: Evans Music-and-Supper Rooms is located just steps from Middle Earth.
claims.evidence: The evidence confirms that Evans Music-and-Supper Rooms is listed as a Wikipedia article, 10m from the same location as Middle Earth, indicating they are in close proximity.

claims.claim: Middle Earth is now invisible to the modern eye but its legacy remains in the area.
claims.evidence: The evidence confirms that Middle Earth (club) is listed as a Wikipedia article, and the story's context suggests its legacy is remembered in the area.

claims.claim: The alleyways of Covent Garden once switched between hymn-singing and hallucinogenic revelry.
claims.evidence: This is a hedged claim about the historical atmosphere of the area, which is plausible but not directly confirmed by the evidence provided.

claims.claim: Middle Earth's memories exist mostly in the listings of Wikipedia and the speculative glances of those who wonder about the area's past.
claims.evidence: The evidence confirms that Middle Earth (club) is listed as a Wikipedia article, and the story's context suggests that its memories are preserved in such listings and local speculation.
